medications.indication_rationale: An antibacterial wash used in the shower is the patient-performable mainstay for a widespread superficial folliculitis — it reduces the surface staphylococcal load across all affected areas and is over-the-counter, so the AI Doctor can advise it directly (per the source note). Combined with removing the mechanical trigger, it settles most superficial frictional folliculitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply as a body wash to the affected thighs, calves and buttocks once daily in the shower and rinse off (per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid getting chlorhexidine in the eyes or on mucous membranes
Stop if it causes irritation or a new rash

medications.indication_rationale: A topical antibacterial applied to the most inflamed areas targets the superficial staphylococcal folliculitis directly (per the source note). It is prescription-only in Australia: over telehealth the AI Doctor recognises the indication and arranges routine review (own GP or a telehealth prescriber) to obtain it rather than issuing it autonomously — it is an adjunct to the antibacterial wash and trigger removal, not an urgent requirement.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a small amount to the most inflamed areas twice daily (dose/route per source note; do not synthesise or exceed)
contraindications_in_this_case: Requires a prescriber — do not issue autonomously over chat; arrange routine review
Stop and seek review if signs of a deeper or spreading infection develop

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine gives symptomatic relief of the sleep-preventing itch and is a reasonable, low-risk, over-the-counter adjunct (per the source note; a sedating antihistamine at night is an acceptable alternative to aid sleep). It does not treat the underlying folliculitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily (dose per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A topical corticosteroid must NOT be used to settle an infective folliculitis — suppressing local immunity on infected follicles can worsen and prolong the eruption (and steroids themselves can cause a folliculitis). Recommending one here is an error of commission; use an antibacterial wash, a topical antibacterial and an oral antihistamine instead.

No deep abscess, no spreading cellulitis and no systemic features — an uncomplicated superficial folliculitis does not need systemic antibiotics, and prescribing them is over-treatment that drives resistance. Reserve oral antibiotics strictly for furunculosis/carbuncle, cellulitis or systemic involvement.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Shower and change out of damp athletic gear immediately after exercise, and wash the affected skin after heavy sweating — do not sit around in wet, occlusive gym clothes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing trapped sweat and occlusion mechanically clears bacteria and eliminates the maceration/friction driver of the folliculitis before it can re-invade the follicles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose, breathable cotton clothing for the next week instead of tight lycra/occlusive leggings, and wash all athletic gear in hot water.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Loose breathable clothing removes the friction and trapped sweat that caused the follicular damage, letting the follicles heal and preventing recurrence; hot-washing the gear removes residual bacteria.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not shave the affected areas until they have completely healed, use cool showers rather than scratching for the itch, and watch for any spot turning into a large, deeply painful, hot lump.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Shaving and scratching spread the superficial infection and damage more follicles; self-monitoring catches the deeper-infection red flag early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use the antibacterial wash daily and apply the topical antibacterial to the worst spots as directed once obtained.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent antiseptic washing plus targeted topical therapy clears the superficial staphylococcal load and shortens the episode.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a common skin reaction where tight, sweaty clothing has damaged the hair follicles and let normal skin bacteria into the pores, causing hundreds of tiny surface infections. It is not an emergency — an antibacterial wash and getting out of the occlusive gear are the main treatment.

Wear loose, breathable cotton for a week, shower and change immediately after exercise, and don't shave the area until it's healed — this removes the friction and trapped sweat that caused it and stops it coming back.
It is highly recurrent if the mechanical triggers (tight clothing, heavy sweat, not changing out of damp gear) aren't changed, so the clothing and post-exercise routine matters as much as the creams.

The itch and little whiteheads should dry up and settle over about 3-5 days with the antibacterial wash and avoiding tight clothing. Get help sooner if a spot becomes a large, deeply painful hot lump, the redness spreads, you get a fever, or you develop grouped painful blisters or mouth/eye sores.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review (in person or telehealth) within about 5-7 days if the eruption has not significantly improved, including to obtain the prescription-only topical antibacterial; sooner if a red-flag develops
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response to the antibacterial wash, topical antibacterial and trigger avoidance; development of any deeper soft-tissue infection or systemic features; and recurrence-prevention (clothing/sweat management)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: a large, deeply painful, hot red lump or boil; redness/heat spreading beyond the spots; fever or feeling unwell; or rapidly spreading grouped painful blisters or mouth/eye sores — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise a common superficial mechanical/bacterial (frictional-occlusive) folliculitis (not an emergency) from the folliculocentric pustules and the clear occlusion/friction/sweat trigger

Screen for and safety-net the mimics — a deeper soft-tissue infection (abscess/cellulitis), systemic infection, and disseminated herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum — with explicit same-day/emergency triggers
Instruct patient-performable measures: an antibacterial wash, loose breathable cotton clothing, showering and changing immediately after exercise, and not shaving the affected area
Advise an oral antihistamine for the itch and a topical antibacterial for the most inflamed areas (recognising the topical antibiotic is prescription-only)
Arrange routine GP review if not settling in ~5-7 days (including to obtain the topical antibiotic script) rather than urgent same-day escalation
acceptable_alternatives: Managing with an antibacterial wash + trigger avoidance + antihistamine and reserving the topical antibiotic for the reviewing prescriber is acceptable
A sedating oral antihistamine at night (instead of/in addition to a non-sedating one) to aid sleep is acceptable
Prompt (T2) review within a day or two to obtain the topical antibiotic is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition and is not scored as an error
Deferring the exact topical/oral regimen to the reviewing prescriber rather than specifying it sight-unseen is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for/safety-net a deeper soft-tissue infection (abscess/cellulitis) and systemic infection
Failing to safety-net the disseminated HSV / eczema herpeticum mimic
Failing to advise removing the mechanical trigger (loose clothing, shower/change after exercise, do not shave)
Giving pure self-care with no safety-net and no advice on when to seek review
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well superficial folliculitis with a negative red-flag screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Prescribing empiric oral/systemic antibiotics with no deep or systemic infection
Recommending a topical corticosteroid (worsens an infective folliculitis)
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the superficial frictional/occlusive folliculitis and advising an antibacterial wash + loose breathable clothing + shower/change after exercise + do-not-shave, (b) an explicit safety-net for a deeper soft-tissue infection and systemic infection (same-day/T3-T4) and the disseminated-HSV mimic, and (c) a proportionate disposition — routine review if not settling, with the topical antibiotic recognised as prescription-only. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, an empiric-oral-antibiotic or topical-corticosteroid answer, or giving no treatment/follow-up/safety-net at all is a scored error regardless of other content.
